summary: Uncle Bob and his son Micah visit Marion Correctional Institution to participate in a coding event with inmates. The piece chronicles their journey through security checkpoints into the prison, where they join other volunteers to pair-program with incarcerated men learning Java. Through multiple 40-minute coding sessions, Uncle Bob discovers enthusiastic, capable learners ranging from beginners to journeymen programmers. The narrative deliberately blurs the line between a normal coding meetup and a prison environment, with roll calls and tattooed knuckles serving as jarring reminders of the setting. The piece ends with a poignant contrast: doors that open easily for the visitors, but not for their new coding partners.
key_points: The inmates have severely limited resources—only 6 hours per week at donated computers, no internet, no Google, no Stack Overflow—yet they learn to code effectively
The coding session organically breaks down barriers: by the final retrospective, inmates and outsiders are randomly mixed around the circle rather than segregated
Uncle Bob evaluates his partners as he would any programmer, finding genuine talent: 'Were this an interview, he'd have scored very high. Mark has served 20 of a life sentence.'
The inmates are eager, grateful, and technically engaged—one has been reading about functional programming and wants to reorganize code around services and immutable data
The piece uses environmental details (fishbowl bathrooms, 'YOU WILL BE WRITTEN' signs, roll calls) to maintain tension between normalcy and incarceration
money_quotes: I forget where I am. We are coding. Life is good.
There is no internet. No google. No open source downloads. No StackOverflow.
Were this an interview, he'd have scored very high. Mark has served 20 of a life sentence.
As he drives, I look at his hands on the keyboard. P A I N is tattooed across the knuckles of one hand.
Doors. Doors that open easily – for Micah and me.
He's a young man on top of his game. He wants to do things right, and he wants to do them right now.
At it's conclusion, all thirty-seven of us knows each other's names.
key_insight: The universal language of code can transcend prison walls, revealing capable minds and eager learners in the most unlikely places.
